1: Psalm 82 is the most theologically concentrated psalm in the collection — the divine council under judgment. אֱלֹהִים נִצָּב בַּעֲדַת-אֵל בְּקֶרֶב אֱלֹהִים יִשְׁפֹּט (ʾĕlōhîm niṣṣāb baʿădat-ʾêl bĕqereb ʾĕlōhîm yishpōṭ, "God has taken his place in the divine assembly; in the midst of the gods he holds judgment!") — niṣṣāb (niphal of nāṣab: standing firmly, taking the presiding judge's position). ʿĂdat-ʾêl: the council/congregation of God (the divine council — the bĕnê ʾĕlōhîm of Genesis 6:2; Job 1:6; Psalms 2:1; Psalms 89:6-7: heavenly beings attending the divine sovereign). Bĕqereb ʾĕlōhîm yishpōṭ: "in the midst of the gods he holds court" — the participial yishpōṭ (ongoing characteristic judging). Whether the ʾĕlōhîm are human judges or divine beings is the central interpretive question: the death-like-Adam of v7 makes greater sense if addressed to beings who considered themselves immortal.
2: עַד-מָתַי תִּשְׁפְּטוּ-עָוֶל (ʿad-mātay tishpĕṭû-ʿāwel, "how long will you go on judging unjustly?") — ʿad-mātay: the "how long" of lament, now in the divine mouth. ʿāwel: injustice, moral perversity (what is crooked). The divine council members who were meant to execute divine justice have done the opposite. וּפְנֵי רְשָׁעִים תִּשְׂאוּ-סֶלָה (ûpĕnê rĕshāʿîm tiśʾû-selāh, "showing partiality to the wicked! Selah.") — nāśāʾ pānîm: "to lift the face" (giving preferential treatment — the exact language Deuteronomy 10:17 uses for what God does NOT do: "who does not lift faces"). Selāh.
3: שִׁפְטוּ-דַל וְיָתוֹם עָנִי וָרָשׁ הַצְדִּיקוּ (shiptû-dal wĕyātôm ʿānî wārāsh haṣdîqû, "defend the poor and fatherless; maintain the right of the afflicted and the destitute!") — four terms for the socially vulnerable: dal (the weak/economically depleted), yātôm (the orphan — without a father-protector), ʿānî (the afflicted), rāsh (the poor). Haṣdîq (hiphil: to declare righteous, to vindicate in court): the positive mandate — the council's job was to be advocates for the voiceless. This is the agenda of Isaiah 1:17; Micah 6:8; and the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:6).
4: פַּלְּטוּ-דַל וְאֶבְיוֹן מִיַּד רְשָׁעִים הַצִּילוּ (palĕṭû-dal wĕʾebyôn miyad rĕshāʿîm haṣṣîlû, "rescue the poor and needy; save them from the grip of the wicked!") — pālaṭ (piel: to cause to escape, to rescue from the narrow place); nāṣal (hiphil: to snatch away to safety). Two rescue verbs paired with two victim terms. Miyad rĕshāʿîm: "from the hand of the wicked" — the hand as the instrument of power and oppression. This is the divine council's actual mandate: to liberate the powerless from the grip of the powerful.
5: לֹא יָדְעוּ וְלֹא יָבִינוּ בַּחֲשֵׁכָה יִתְהַלָּכוּ (lōʾ yādĕʿû wĕlōʾ yābînû baḥăshêkāh yithallākû, "they have no knowledge and no understanding; they walk about in darkness!") — the triple failure: no knowledge (lōʾ yādĕʿû), no understanding (lōʾ yābînû), walking in darkness (baḥăshêkāh). The council-members who should dispense divine wisdom are themselves without wisdom and in darkness. יִמּוֹטוּ כָּל-מוֹסְדֵי אָרֶץ (yimmôṭû kol-môsĕdê ʾāreṣ, "all the foundations of the earth are shaken!") — môsĕdîm: foundations (Psalms 18:15; Job 38:4). The moral failure of the heavenly council reverberates cosmically: when justice fails at the heavenly level, the earth's foundations themselves become unstable (Proverbs 2:21-22; Isaiah 24:18-19).
6: אֲנִי-אָמַרְתִּי אֱלֹהִים אַתֶּם וּבְנֵי עֶלְיוֹן כֻּלְּכֶם (ʾănî-ʾāmartî ʾĕlōhîm ʾattem ûbĕnê ʿelyôn kullĕkem, "I declared, 'You are gods, sons of the Most High, every one of you!'") — the divine "I" recalling a prior declaration: God himself conferred the status ʾĕlōhîm and bĕnê ʿelyôn (sons of the Most High) on the council-members. This is the foundation from which v7's reversal is so devastating. Jesus cites this verse in John 10:34-35: "Is it not written in your Law, 'I said, you are gods'? If he called them gods to whom the word of God came — and Scripture cannot be broken — how can you say that the one the Father consecrated and sent into the world is blaspheming by saying 'I am God's Son'?" The argument moves from the lesser to the greater: if the divine council received "gods," how much more the one God consecrated and sent.
7: אָכֵן כְּאָדָם תְּמוּתוּן וּכְאַחַד הַשָּׂרִים תִּפֹּלוּ (ʾāken kĕʾādām tĕmûtûn ûkĕʾaḥad haśśārîm tippōlû, "nevertheless, you will die like the man [Adam]/like human beings, and fall like any fallen ruler!") — ʾāken: "nevertheless, however" — the adversative that shatters the status of v6. Kĕʾādām: "like Adam" or "like a human" — the polysemy is likely intentional: both readings operate simultaneously. To die like Adam is to share the curse of Genesis 3:19 ("you are dust, and to dust you shall return") — the beings designated "gods" will share the fate of the one who grasped for divine status and received mortality instead. Kĕʾaḥad haśśārîm tippōlû: "like one of the rulers you will fall" — Isaiah 14:12-15 (the fall of the king of Babylon). The ultimate theodicy: the unjust divine council will be subject to death itself.
8: קוּמָה אֱלֹהִים שָׁפְטָה הָאָרֶץ (qûmāh ʾĕlōhîm shāpĕṭāh hāʾāreṣ, "Rise up, O God, judge the earth!") — the Psalmist's voice returns for the first time since v1. Qûm (the warrior's arising — Psalms 68:1: "let God arise, let his enemies be scattered"): the call for God to actively judge replaces the failed council. כִּי-אַתָּה תִנְחַל בְּכָל-הַגּוֹיִם (kî-ʾattāh tinḥal bĕkhol-haggôyim, "for all the nations belong to you!") — nāḥal: to inherit, to take possession. The psalm ends on a massive claim: not just Israel but all the nations are God's inheritance. The unjust sub-rulers replaced by the one just Sovereign who owns every nation — the universal scope resolves the crisis of the psalm.
